$comment: Module 2 - Quantum Gates & Circuits question bank. One item per objective, tagged by objective_id. Grading matches apps.quizzes.views: mcq/true_false compare lowercased answer to the unique is_correct option id; numerical uses |ans-correct| <= |correct|*tolerance. JSON backslashes single-escaped -> one literal backslash for KaTeX.
title: Module 2 - Quantum Gates & Circuits

quizzes.description: Pauli/Hadamard/phase gates, rotation operators, Bloch-sphere action, unitarity and inverses, and commutation.

questions.text: In the computational basis, which is the correct matrix of the Pauli-$Y$ gate?
questions.hint: $Y = iXZ$; its off-diagonal entries carry $\mp i$ with the $-i$ in the top-right.
questions.explanation: $Y=\begin{pmatrix}0&-i\\ i&0\end{pmatrix}$. Option (b) is $X$, (c) is $Z$, and (d) has the signs of $Y$ transposed.

questions.text: Applying the Hadamard gate to $|1\rangle$ gives which state?
questions.hint: $H=\tfrac{1}{\sqrt2}\begin{pmatrix}1&1\\ 1&-1\end{pmatrix}$ acting on the column $(0,1)^T$.
questions.explanation: $H|1\rangle=\tfrac{1}{\sqrt2}(|0\rangle-|1\rangle)=|-\rangle$. $H|0\rangle$ gives the $+$ combination.

questions.text: Writing $R_z(\theta)=e^{-i\theta Z/2}$, its matrix is:
questions.hint: Diagonalize in the $Z$-eigenbasis: $e^{-i\theta Z/2}$ acts as $e^{\mp i\theta/2}$ on $|0\rangle,|1\rangle$.
questions.explanation: Because $Z|0\rangle=+|0\rangle$ and $Z|1\rangle=-|1\rangle$, $R_z(\theta)=\mathrm{diag}(e^{-i\theta/2},e^{+i\theta/2})$. Option (c) is the phase gate $P(\theta)$, which differs from $R_z$ by a global phase.

questions.text: On the Bloch sphere, the Hadamard gate acts as a rotation by $\pi$ about which axis?
questions.hint: $H$ swaps $\hat x\leftrightarrow\hat z$ and negates $\hat y$; that is a $\pi$ rotation about the diagonal in the $xz$-plane.
questions.explanation: $H=\tfrac{1}{\sqrt2}(X+Z)$, so it is a $\pi$ rotation about $(\hat x+\hat z)/\sqrt2$: it maps $|0\rangle\!\to\!|+\rangle$ (north pole to the $+x$ equator) and exchanges the $x$ and $z$ axes.

options.text: the $\hat x+\hat z$ axis (i.e. $(\hat x+\hat z)/\sqrt2$)

questions.text: The $T$ gate is $\mathrm{diag}(1,e^{i\pi/4})$. Its inverse $T^{\dagger}$ is:
questions.hint: For a unitary, $U^{\dagger}=U^{-1}$; for a diagonal gate that conjugates each phase.
questions.explanation: $T$ is unitary, so $T^{\dagger}=\mathrm{diag}(1,e^{-i\pi/4})$ and $T T^{\dagger}=I$. Note $T^2=S$ and $T^8=I$.

questions.text: Do the Pauli gates $X$ and $Z$ commute, and what is $[X,Z]=XZ-ZX$?
questions.hint: Use $XZ=-iY$ and $ZX=+iY$.
questions.explanation: $XZ=-iY$ and $ZX=iY$, so $\{X,Z\}=0$ (anticommute) and $[X,Z]=XZ-ZX=-2iY\neq0$. Distinct Paulis always anticommute.

quizzes.description: CNOT/CZ/SWAP action, entanglement generation, phase kickback, and the SWAP = 3 CNOT identity.

questions.text: With the first qubit as control and the second as target, CNOT maps $|10\rangle$ to:
questions.hint: CNOT flips the target iff the control is $|1\rangle$.
questions.explanation: Control $=1$, so the target flips $0\to1$: $|10\rangle\to|11\rangle$. On $|0x\rangle$ the target is unchanged.

questions.text: What is $\mathrm{SWAP}\,|01\rangle$?
questions.hint: SWAP exchanges the two qubits' values.
questions.explanation: SWAP exchanges the basis labels: $|01\rangle\to|10\rangle$. It fixes $|00\rangle$ and $|11\rangle$.

questions.text: Applying $H$ to the first qubit of $|00\rangle$ and then CNOT (control = first) produces:
questions.hint: $H$ makes $\tfrac{1}{\sqrt2}(|0\rangle+|1\rangle)|0\rangle$; CNOT copies the control into the target.
questions.explanation: $H\otimes I:|00\rangle\to\tfrac{1}{\sqrt2}(|00\rangle+|10\rangle)$, then CNOT flips the target on the $|1\rangle$ branch giving the Bell state $|\Phi^+\rangle=\tfrac{1}{\sqrt2}(|00\rangle+|11\rangle)$.

questions.text: Applying CNOT (control = first) to the product state $|+\rangle|0\rangle$ yields a state that is:
questions.hint: Write $|+\rangle|0\rangle=\tfrac{1}{\sqrt2}(|00\rangle+|10\rangle)$ and apply CNOT.
questions.explanation: CNOT gives $\tfrac{1}{\sqrt2}(|00\rangle+|11\rangle)$, which cannot be factored as a tensor product — it is maximally entangled.

questions.text: Phase kickback refers to which effect of a controlled-$U$ when the target is an eigenstate $|u\rangle$ with $U|u\rangle=e^{i\phi}|u\rangle$?
questions.hint: $C\text{-}U\,(\alpha|0\rangle+\beta|1\rangle)|u\rangle = (\alpha|0\rangle+\beta e^{i\phi}|1\rangle)|u\rangle$.
questions.explanation: Because $|u\rangle$ is an eigenstate, controlled-$U$ multiplies only the $|1\rangle_{\text{control}}$ branch by $e^{i\phi}$: the phase 'kicks back' to the control while the target is untouched. This drives Deutsch-Jozsa and phase estimation.

options.text: the eigenphase $e^{i\phi}$ is transferred onto the control qubit's amplitude, leaving $|u\rangle$ unchanged
the target is measured and collapses
the control and target are swapped
the target acquires a bit flip

questions.text: The identity $\mathrm{SWAP}=\mathrm{CNOT}_{1\to2}\,\mathrm{CNOT}_{2\to1}\,\mathrm{CNOT}_{1\to2}$ holds (three alternating CNOTs implement SWAP).
questions.hint: Check it on the four computational-basis states, or recall the standard three-CNOT SWAP.
questions.explanation: True. Three CNOTs alternating control/target exchange the two qubits; e.g. on $|10\rangle$: $\to|11\rangle\to|01\rangle\to|01\rangle$, matching SWAP.

quizzes.description: Wires/gates/time-ordering, composition as matrix products, tensor products on parallel wires, and circuit identities.

questions.text: In the quantum circuit model, which statement is correct?
questions.hint: Gates are unitary and reversible; time flows left to right in the diagram.
questions.explanation: Each wire is a qubit; gates are unitaries applied in temporal order (drawn left to right); no-cloning forbids copying; measurement extracts classical outcomes at the end.

options.text: wires carry qubits and gates (unitaries) act left-to-right in time; measurement typically ends a wire
wires carry classical bits only and gates may be irreversible
gates act right-to-left in time and can clone qubits
every gate must act on all qubits simultaneously

questions.text: A circuit applies gate $A$, then $B$, then $C$ (in time). The overall unitary is:
questions.hint: Later-in-time operators sit to the left when multiplying a column vector.
questions.explanation: Acting on $|\psi\rangle$: first $A$, then $B$, then $C$ gives $C(B(A|\psi\rangle))=CBA|\psi\rangle$. Time order reverses in the matrix product.

questions.text: $H$ acts on the top wire and $X$ on the bottom wire in the same time slice. The two-qubit operator is:
questions.hint: Parallel (simultaneous) gates on distinct wires combine by tensor product, top wire first.
questions.explanation: Gates on disjoint wires in the same layer tensor together: top$\,\otimes\,$bottom $=H\otimes X$, a $4\times4$ unitary.

questions.text: The circuit identity $HXH$ equals which single-qubit gate?
questions.hint: $H$ conjugation exchanges $X\leftrightarrow Z$.
questions.explanation: $HXH=Z$ (and $HZH=X$): the Hadamard interchanges the $X$ and $Z$ bases, so conjugating $X$ by $H$ yields $Z$.

questions.text: Two circuits on the same $n$ qubits are guaranteed to implement the same operation iff:
questions.hint: Equality on one input is necessary but not sufficient; equality of the full unitary is.
questions.explanation: Two circuits are equivalent exactly when their unitaries match (a global phase is unobservable). Agreeing on one basis state, or having equal gate count/depth, does not guarantee equal action on superpositions.

options.text: their overall unitary matrices are equal (up to an irrelevant global phase)
they use the same number of gates
they agree on the single input $|0\dots0\rangle$
they have the same circuit depth

quizzes.description: Universality definitions, exact vs approximate, Solovay-Kitaev scaling, exact gate synthesis, and Gottesman-Knill.

questions.text: Which of the following is a standard universal gate set for quantum computation?
questions.hint: You need a non-Clifford gate to escape the Clifford group.
questions.explanation: $\{H,T,\mathrm{CNOT}\}$ is universal: it approximates any unitary to arbitrary accuracy. $\{H,S,\mathrm{CNOT}\}$ is Clifford (not universal); $\{X,Z,\mathrm{CNOT}\}$ is even weaker.

questions.text: What distinguishes exact universality from approximate universality?
questions.hint: A finite gate set generates only a countable group, so it cannot hit every (uncountable) unitary exactly.
questions.explanation: A finite set like $\{H,T,\mathrm{CNOT}\}$ generates a dense but countable subgroup, giving approximate universality (any $\epsilon$). Exact universality needs a continuum of gates (e.g. all single-qubit rotations plus CNOT).

options.text: an exactly universal set generates every unitary precisely; an approximately universal (finite) set can reach any unitary only to within arbitrary $\epsilon$
approximate universality requires infinitely many qubits
exact universality forbids the use of CNOT

questions.text: The Solovay-Kitaev theorem says any single-qubit unitary can be approximated to accuracy $\epsilon$ using how many gates from a universal set?
questions.hint: Solovay-Kitaev gives polylogarithmic overhead in $1/\epsilon$.
questions.explanation: Solovay-Kitaev: $O(\log^{c}(1/\epsilon))$ gates, with a small constant $c\approx2$ (as quoted by Nielsen & Chuang; the basic Dawson-Nielsen construction gives $\approx4$). The overhead in accuracy is only polylogarithmic, which is why finite gate sets are practical.

questions.text: Using only the $T$ gate ($T=\mathrm{diag}(1,e^{i\pi/4})$), the Pauli-$Z$ gate is realized exactly as $T^{n}$. Give the smallest positive integer $n$.
questions.hint: $Z=\mathrm{diag}(1,e^{i\pi})$; each $T$ adds a phase of $\pi/4$ to the $|1\rangle$ amplitude.
questions.explanation: $T^n=\mathrm{diag}(1,e^{in\pi/4})$; setting $e^{in\pi/4}=e^{i\pi}=-1$ needs $n=4$. (Also $T^2=S$ and $T^8=I$.)

questions.text: By the Gottesman-Knill theorem, why is the Clifford group $\{H,S,\mathrm{CNOT}\}$ not universal?
questions.hint: Gottesman-Knill: stabilizer (Clifford) circuits admit efficient classical simulation.
questions.explanation: Gottesman-Knill shows Clifford circuits (on stabilizer inputs, with computational-basis measurement) are classically efficiently simulable, so they cannot be universal. Adding any non-Clifford gate such as $T$ restores universality. (Clifford gates do create entanglement, e.g. CNOT.)

options.text: Clifford circuits are efficiently simulable on a classical computer, so alone they cannot achieve quantum advantage; a non-Clifford gate (e.g. $T$) is required

quizzes.description: Density-matrix properties, purity, partial trace, measurement statistics from rho, and the Bloch-ball picture.

questions.text: Which set of properties characterizes a valid density matrix $\rho$?
questions.hint: Probabilities live on the diagonal (sum to 1, non-negative) in every basis.
questions.explanation: $\rho=\sum_i p_i|\psi_i\rangle\langle\psi_i|$ is Hermitian, unit-trace, and positive semidefinite. These guarantee real non-negative outcome probabilities summing to 1.

questions.text: For the pure state $|+\rangle$, the density matrix is $\rho=\tfrac12\begin{pmatrix}1&1\\1&1\end{pmatrix}$. Compute the purity $\mathrm{Tr}(\rho^2)$.
questions.hint: Pure states satisfy $\rho^2=\rho$, hence $\mathrm{Tr}(\rho^2)=\mathrm{Tr}\,\rho$.
questions.explanation: $\rho^2=\rho$ for any pure state, so $\mathrm{Tr}(\rho^2)=\mathrm{Tr}\,\rho=1$. Purity $=1$ certifies a pure state.

questions.text: The maximally mixed single-qubit state is $\rho=I/2$. Compute its purity $\mathrm{Tr}(\rho^2)$.
questions.hint: $\rho^2=(I/2)^2=I/4$; take the trace over a 2-dimensional space.
questions.explanation: $\mathrm{Tr}((I/2)^2)=\mathrm{Tr}(I/4)=2\cdot\tfrac14=\tfrac12$. For a qubit, purity ranges from $1$ (pure) down to $1/2$ (maximally mixed).

questions.text: Take the Bell state $|\Phi^+\rangle=\tfrac{1}{\sqrt2}(|00\rangle+|11\rangle)$. The reduced density matrix of qubit A (partial trace over B) is:
questions.hint: Trace out B: $\rho_A=\tfrac12(|0\rangle\langle0|+|1\rangle\langle1|)$.
questions.explanation: $\rho_A=\mathrm{Tr}_B|\Phi^+\rangle\langle\Phi^+|=\tfrac12(|0\rangle\langle0|+|1\rangle\langle1|)=I/2$. Maximal entanglement makes each local state maximally mixed.

questions.text: For $\rho=\mathrm{diag}(0.75,\,0.25)$ in the computational basis, compute the expectation value $\langle Z\rangle=\mathrm{Tr}(\rho Z)$.
questions.hint: $\langle Z\rangle=\rho_{00}-\rho_{11}$ for a diagonal $\rho$.
questions.explanation: $\mathrm{Tr}(\rho Z)=0.75(+1)+0.25(-1)=0.5$. Diagonal entries are the outcome probabilities $P(0),P(1)$.

questions.text: Writing $\rho=(I+\vec r\cdot\vec\sigma)/2$ with Bloch vector $\vec r$, a state with $|\vec r|=0.6$ is:
questions.hint: Pure $\Leftrightarrow |\vec r|=1$ (surface); $|\vec r|=0$ is maximally mixed (center).
questions.explanation: $|\vec r|=1$ is the pure-state sphere; $0\le|\vec r|<1$ is a mixed state inside the ball. $|\vec r|=0.6$ is a valid mixed state with purity $\tfrac12(1+|\vec r|^2)=0.68$.

options.text: mixed and strictly inside the Bloch ball ($|\vec r|<1$)
pure and on the Bloch sphere
